abstract: We introduce Wan-Animate, a unified framework for character animation and replacement. Given a character image and a reference video, Wan-Animate can animate the character by precisely replicating the expressions and movements of the character in the video to generate high-fidelity character videos. Alternatively, it can integrate the animated character into the reference video to replace the original character, replicating the scene's lighting and color tone to achieve seamless environmental integration. Wan-Animate is built upon the Wan model. To adapt it for character animation tasks, we employ a modified input paradigm to differentiate between reference conditions and regions for generation. This design unifies multiple tasks into a common symbolic representation. We use spatially-aligned skeleton signals to replicate body motion and implicit facial features extracted from source images to reenact expressions, enabling the generation of character videos with high controllability and expressiveness. Furthermore, to enhance environmental integration during character replacement, we develop an auxiliary Relighting LoRA. This module preserves the character's appearance consistency while applying the appropriate environmental lighting and color tone. Experimental results demonstrate that Wan-Animate achieves state-of-the-art performance. We are committed to open-sourcing the model weights and its source code.

keyInsights: A DiT-based architecture can effectively unify complex character animation and replacement tasks within a single framework through a specialized input formulation.
Decoupling body and facial control signals allows for high-fidelity and expressive character animation, with dedicated modules improving performance for each aspect.
An auxiliary Relighting LoRA is crucial for achieving seamless environmental integration by adapting the animated character's lighting and color to the target scene without compromising identity.
originalProblem: Existing character animation methods often lack a holistic framework for simultaneously controlling body motion, facial expressions, and seamless environmental interaction.
Current open-source solutions frequently suffer from lower generation quality, realism, and temporal smoothness, especially those based on older UNet architectures.
Many advanced open-source projects primarily focus on body motion control, often neglecting expressive facial dynamics or comprehensive character replacement capabilities.
results: WAN-ANIMATE achieved superior quantitative performance on self-reconstruction tasks compared to open-source competitors, with SSIM scores of 0.813 for general animation and 0.834 for facial animation.

Human evaluations showed participants preferred WAN-ANIMATE over commercial products like Runway's Act-two and Bytedance's DreamActor-M1 in terms of overall video quality, identity consistency, and motion/expression accuracy.
Ablation studies confirmed the necessity of both the progressive training pipeline for robust face control and the Relighting LoRA for effective environmental fusion in replacement scenarios.
solution: WAN-ANIMATE is built upon the DiT-based Wan-I2V model, utilizing a modified input paradigm to unify diverse tasks such as character animation and replacement.
It employs decoupled control signals for body motion (using a 2D skeleton representation) and facial expressions (using raw facial images with a disentangling Face Adapter).
An auxiliary Relighting LoRA module is incorporated to adjust the animated character's lighting and color tone, ensuring seamless integration into new environments during character replacement.
paper_summary.summary: Alibaba's HumanAIGC Team developed WAN-ANIMATE, a unified framework for character animation and replacement that delivers high-fidelity results. The open-sourced model outperforms existing open-source alternatives and is preferred over commercial products like Runway's Act-two and Bytedance's DreamActor-M1 in human evaluations across metrics like identity consistency and expression accuracy.

title: Wan-Animate: Unified Character Animation and Replacement with Holistic Replication

abstract:
Video try-on replaces clothing in videos with target garments. Existing
methods struggle to generate high-quality and temporally consistent results
when handling complex clothing patterns and diverse body poses. We present
3DV-TON, a novel diffusion-based framework for generating high-fidelity and
temporally consistent video try-on results. Our approach employs generated
animatable textured 3D meshes as explicit frame-level guidance, alleviating the
issue of models over-focusing on appearance fidelity at the expanse of motion
coherence. This is achieved by enabling direct reference to consistent garment
texture movements throughout video sequences. The proposed method features an
adaptive pipeline for generating dynamic 3D guidance: (1) selecting a keyframe
for initial 2D image try-on, followed by (2) reconstructing and animating a
textured 3D mesh synchronized with original video poses. We further introduce a
robust rectangular masking strategy that successfully mitigates artifact
propagation caused by leaking clothing information during dynamic human and
garment movements. To advance video try-on research, we introduce HR-VVT, a
high-resolution benchmark dataset containing 130 videos with diverse clothing
types and scenarios. Quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate our
superior performance over existing methods. The project page is at this link
this https URL

keyInsights: Explicit 3D guidance provides better temporal consistency than 2D warping approaches
Combining textured 3D meshes with diffusion models enables both detail preservation and smooth motion
Dynamic rectangular masking prevents garment information leakage during human motion
originalProblem: Existing video try-on methods struggle with temporal consistency and realistic garment motion
Current approaches have difficulty handling complex clothing patterns and diverse body poses
Trade-off between appearance fidelity and motion coherence in video generation
results: Demonstrated superior performance in handling complex clothing and diverse poses
Achieved significantly reduced flickering and warping artifacts compared to existing methods
Created more realistic and stable clothing motion while maintaining high-resolution details
Introduced HR-VVT dataset for evaluating high-resolution video try-on performance
solution: Reconstruct textured 3D meshes from single-frame try-on results to guide video generation
Use diffusion models with temporal attention and dynamic rectangular masking
Implement adaptive keyframe selection and clothing-specific feature extraction
paper_summary.summary: A framework combining textured 3D mesh guidance with diffusion models enables high-fidelity video try-on by reconstructing animated 3D avatars from single-frame try-on results, achieving superior temporal consistency and garment detail preservation compared to existing methods while introducing the HR-VVT dataset for evaluation of high-resolution video try-on.

title: 3DV-TON: Textured 3D-Guided Consistent Video Try-on via Diffusion Models

abstract: We present Concat-ID, a unified framework for identity-preserving video generation. Concat-ID employs variational autoencoders to extract image features, which are then concatenated with video latents along the sequence dimension. It relies exclusively on inherent 3D self-attention mechanisms to incorporate them, eliminating the need for additional parameters or modules. A novel cross-video pairing strategy and a multi-stage training regimen are introduced to balance identity consistency and facial editability while enhancing video naturalness. Extensive experiments demonstrate Concat-ID's superiority over existing methods in both single and multi-identity generation, as well as its seamless scalability to multi-subject scenarios, including virtual try-on and background-controllable generation. Concat-ID establishes a new benchmark for identity-preserving video synthesis, providing a versatile and scalable solution for a wide range of applications.

keyInsights: Concatenating identity features in the sequence dimension within the video latent space allows existing 3D self-attention to naturally learn identity preservation without specialized modules or additional parameters.
Sourcing reference images from different but identity-matched videos during training (cross-video pairing) is crucial for disentangling identity from expression and enhancing facial editability.

A progressive multi-stage training strategy, moving from self-supervised identity learning to fine-tuning with cross-video and trade-off pairs, effectively balances identity consistency and facial editability.
originalProblem: Maintaining consistent identity in generated videos while allowing for diverse facial expressions and actions, often presenting a trade-off for existing methods.
Prior identity-preserving video synthesis approaches rely on complex architectures, introducing additional parameters, specialized modules, or auxiliary loss functions.
Scalability limitations for multi-identity or multi-subject video generation, often requiring task-specific architectural redesigns.
results: Achieved superior identity consistency (ArcSim: 0.442) and facial editability (CLIPDist: 0.325) in single-identity generation compared to state-of-the-art baselines like ConsisID and ID-Animator.
Demonstrated strong scalability to multi-identity and multi-subject (e.g., virtual try-on) video generation scenarios without requiring architectural modifications or additional parameters.
User studies confirmed Concat-ID's superiority in identity consistency, facial motion alignment, and naturalness over the strongest baseline, ConsisID.
solution: A unified architecture that concatenates VAE-encoded reference image features with video latents along the sequence dimension, leveraging existing 3D self-attention mechanisms without new modules.
A three-tiered data construction strategy (pre-training, cross-video, trade-off pairs) using filtering and cross-video pairing to progressively balance identity consistency and facial editability.
A multi-stage training regimen that progressively optimizes the model on these datasets to achieve an optimal balance of consistency, editability, and naturalness.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Renmin University of China, Tsinghua University, and Zhipu AI developed Concat-ID, a framework for identity-preserving video synthesis that leverages sequence-wise concatenation of VAE-encoded image features and existing 3D self-attention mechanisms. The method achieves superior identity consistency (ArcSim of 0.442 for single-identity) and facial editability (CLIPDist of 0.325) across single, multi-identity, and multi-subject scenarios without adding new modules or parameters, outperforming existing baselines as confirmed by quantitative metrics and user studies.

title: Concat-ID: Towards Universal Identity-Preserving Video Synthesis

abstract: We present Dress&Dance, a video diffusion framework that generates high quality 5-second-long 24 FPS virtual try-on videos at 1152x720 resolution of a user wearing desired garments while moving in accordance with a given reference video. Our approach requires a single user image and supports a range of tops, bottoms, and one-piece garments, as well as simultaneous tops and bottoms try-on in a single pass. Key to our framework is CondNet, a novel conditioning network that leverages attention to unify multi-modal inputs (text, images, and videos), thereby enhancing garment registration and motion fidelity. CondNet is trained on heterogeneous training data, combining limited video data and a larger, more readily available image dataset, in a multistage progressive manner. Dress&Dance outperforms existing open source and commercial solutions and enables a high quality and flexible try-on experience.
title: Dress&Dance: Dress up and Dance as You Like It - Technical Preview

abstract: Preserving first-frame identity while ensuring precise motion control is a fundamental challenge in human image animation. The Image-to-Motion Binding process of the dominant Reference-to-Video (R2V) paradigm overlooks critical spatio-temporal misalignments common in real-world applications, leading to failures such as identity drift and visual artifacts. We introduce SteadyDancer, an Image-to-Video (I2V) paradigm-based framework that achieves harmonized and coherent animation and is the first to ensure first-frame preservation robustly. Firstly, we propose a Condition-Reconciliation Mechanism to harmonize the two conflicting conditions, enabling precise control without sacrificing fidelity. Secondly, we design Synergistic Pose Modulation Modules to generate an adaptive and coherent pose representation that is highly compatible with the reference image. Finally, we employ a Staged Decoupled-Objective Training Pipeline that hierarchically optimizes the model for motion fidelity, visual quality, and temporal coherence. Experiments demonstrate that SteadyDancer achieves state-of-the-art performance in both appearance fidelity and motion control, while requiring significantly fewer training resources than comparable methods.

keyInsights: The I2V paradigm can be effectively realized with robust first-frame preservation by carefully reconciling static appearance and dynamic motion information through a multi-faceted conditioning approach.
Deconstructing and adaptively refining pose representations both spatially and temporally is crucial for mitigating real-world misalignments and achieving coherent animation from diverse inputs.

A staged training approach, decoupling objectives such as initial motion control and subsequent quality enhancement, significantly improves training efficiency and stability for complex generative video models.
originalProblem: Existing Reference-to-Video (R2V) human animation methods often suffer from identity drift, visual artifacts, and temporal incoherence due to relaxed alignment constraints between static images and dynamic poses.

The Image-to-Video (I2V) paradigm, which guarantees first-frame preservation, faces challenges in reconciling static appearance with dynamic motion and handling spatio-temporal misalignments between the reference image and driving pose.
High-quality diffusion-based animation models typically demand extensive datasets and computational resources for stable and efficient training, making their development resource-intensive.
results: Achieved an FVD of 326.49 on the RealisDance-Val dataset and 451.3 on the TikTok dataset, outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods in video fidelity and temporal coherence.

Demonstrated superior performance on the novel X-Dance benchmark, consistently generating harmonized and coherent results with near-perfect first-frame identity and precise motion control even with severe spatio-temporal misalignments.

Trained with substantially fewer steps (14,500 total) and a smaller proprietary dataset (7,338 videos, 10.2 hours) compared to other Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based methods, indicating high resource efficiency.
solution: A Condition-Reconciliation Mechanism uses channel-wise concatenation, parameter-efficient injection, and condition augmentation to fuse appearance and pose signals without compromising first-frame fidelity.

Synergistic Pose Modulation Modules (Spatial Structure Adaptive Refiner, Temporal Motion Coherence Module, Frame-wise Attention Alignment Unit) are designed to robustly resolve spatio-temporal misalignments between the reference image and driving pose.

A Staged Decoupled-Objective Training Pipeline, including Action Supervision, Condition-Decoupled Distillation, and Motion Discontinuity Mitigation (via Pose Simulation), ensures efficient and stable training with fewer resources.
paper_summary.summary: SteadyDancer introduces an Image-to-Video (I2V) framework for human image animation that prioritizes first-frame preservation and precise motion control by reconciling conflicting appearance and motion signals. The model achieves state-of-the-art video fidelity and temporal coherence, with an FVD of 326.49 on RealisDance-Val, while requiring significantly fewer training resources.

title: SteadyDancer: Harmonized and Coherent Human Image Animation with First-Frame Preservation

abstract:
Character Animation aims to generating character videos from still images
through driving signals. Currently, diffusion models have become the mainstream
in visual generation research, owing to their robust generative capabilities.
However, challenges persist in the realm of image-to-video, especially in
character animation, where temporally maintaining consistency with detailed
information from character remains a formidable problem. In this paper, we
leverage the power of diffusion models and propose a novel framework tailored
for character animation. To preserve consistency of intricate appearance
features from reference image, we design ReferenceNet to merge detail features
via spatial attention. To ensure controllability and continuity, we introduce
an efficient pose guider to direct character's movements and employ an
effective temporal modeling approach to ensure smooth inter-frame transitions
between video frames. By expanding the training data, our approach can animate
arbitrary characters, yielding superior results in character animation compared
to other image-to-video methods. Furthermore, we evaluate our method on
benchmarks for fashion video and human dance synthesis, achieving
state-of-the-art results.

keyInsights: ReferenceNet, designed with an SD-pretrained UNet-like structure and a spatial-attention mechanism, effectively preserves fine-grained details from a non-aligned reference image, outperforming CLIP or ControlNet for appearance consistency.
A two-stage training strategy, separating image-level detail learning from video-level temporal coherence, is crucial for achieving high-quality frame generation before refining temporal dynamics.

The strategic integration of temporal layers after spatial-attention and cross-attention components within the UNet allows the model to maintain temporal continuity and smoothness while leveraging pre-trained image generation capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing image-to-video synthesis methods struggle to maintain fine-grained appearance consistency and prevent temporal variations (jitter/flickering) in generated character animations.
Prior approaches often lack robust controllability, making it difficult to precisely guide character movements using desired pose sequences.
Many character animation techniques exhibit limited generalizability, often requiring per-sample finetuning or failing to animate arbitrary characters robustly across different styles.
results: Achieves state-of-the-art quantitative results on fashion video, human dance, and talking gesture generation benchmarks (e.g., FVD 81.6 on UBC Fashion Video Dataset, outperforming SD-I2V at 175.4).
Successfully animates a wide range of arbitrary characters (full-body humans, cartoons, etc.) with high definition and realistic detail, demonstrating strong generalizability.
Generates videos with impressive spatial and temporal consistency, maintaining character appearance and smooth motion even during substantial movements, effectively addressing inter-frame jitter.
solution: Extends the Stable Diffusion UNet architecture with a ReferenceNet for appearance consistency, a lightweight Pose Guider for controllability, and temporal layers for continuity.

ReferenceNet uses a UNet-like structure and a novel spatial-attention mechanism to integrate high-fidelity details from the reference image, while the Pose Guider adds processed pose information to the noise latent.
Employs a two-stage training strategy: first training for image-level quality (excluding temporal layers), then introducing and training temporal layers for video-level smoothness.
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents "Animate Anyone," a new method for consistent and controllable image-to-video synthesis that animates arbitrary characters from single still images, guided by desired pose sequences. The approach demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on various human video synthesis benchmarks, significantly improving temporal consistency and appearance preservation compared to prior methods.

title:
Animate Anyone: Consistent and Controllable Image-to-Video Synthesis for
  Character Animation

abstract: Virtual try-on aims to synthesize a realistic image of a person wearing a target garment, but accurately modeling garment-body correspondence remains a persistent challenge, especially under pose and appearance variation. In this paper, we propose Voost - a unified and scalable framework that jointly learns virtual try-on and try-off with a single diffusion transformer. By modeling both tasks jointly, Voost enables each garment-person pair to supervise both directions and supports flexible conditioning over generation direction and garment category, enhancing garment-body relational reasoning without task-specific networks, auxiliary losses, or additional labels. In addition, we introduce two inference-time techniques: attention temperature scaling for robustness to resolution or mask variation, and self-corrective sampling that leverages bidirectional consistency between tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Voost achieves state-of-the-art results on both try-on and try-off benchmarks, consistently outperforming strong baselines in alignment accuracy, visual fidelity, and generalization.

keyInsights: Jointly training a single model for both virtual try-on and try-off tasks enables mutual supervision, intrinsically strengthening garment-body relational reasoning and leading to more coherent and photorealistic results.

Fine-tuning only the attention modules of a pretrained Diffusion Transformer is an effective and computationally efficient strategy to adapt large generative models for specific, detailed tasks like fashion synthesis.

Inference-time techniques, such as attention temperature scaling and self-corrective sampling, significantly enhance model robustness and consistency, especially when dealing with varying resolutions, mask sizes, or complex garment structures not perfectly matched to training conditions.
originalProblem: Existing virtual try-on (VTON) methods struggle with accurate garment-person correspondence, leading to dispersed attention patterns and poor visual fidelity, especially with varied poses or occlusions.
Virtual try-off (VTOFF), the inverse task of reconstructing a clean garment from a dressed person, is significantly underexplored and often requires separate, specialized models.
Current approaches frequently rely on task-specific networks, auxiliary losses, or additional annotations, limiting scalability and generalization across diverse fashion imagery and tasks.
results: Voost achieves state-of-the-art performance on VTON and VTOFF across multiple benchmarks (e.g., VITON-HD, DressCode), outperforming baselines in realism (FID, KID) and structural consistency (SSIM, LPIPS).

Attention maps generated by Voost demonstrate sharply localized and semantically consistent garment-body alignment, indicating superior relational reasoning compared to prior methods with dispersed attention patterns.

Ablation studies confirm that dual-task training substantially improves performance over single-task models, and fine-tuning only attention modules achieves optimal results with fewer trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning.
solution: Voost employs a unified Diffusion Transformer (DiT) that processes both try-on and try-off tasks bidirectionally via a horizontally concatenated input and task-specific binary masking.
A task token is introduced to flexibly condition the model on generation direction (on/off) and garment category (upper, lower, full-body), enabling multitask learning within a single architecture.

The DiT backbone is efficiently fine-tuned by updating only the attention modules, preserving the strong generative prior while minimizing computational cost, and enhanced with inference-time techniques like attention temperature scaling and self-corrective sampling.
paper_summary.summary: Voost, developed by NXN Labs and the University of British Columbia, introduces a unified and scalable Diffusion Transformer that jointly performs virtual try-on and try-off, setting new state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks like VITON-HD and DressCode through enhanced garment-body relational reasoning.

title: Voost: A Unified and Scalable Diffusion Transformer for Bidirectional Virtual Try-On and Try-Off

abstract:
We present a novel method for reconstructing personalized 3D human avatars
with realistic animation from only a few images. Due to the large variations in
body shapes, poses, and cloth types, existing methods mostly require hours of
per-subject optimization during inference, which limits their practical
applications. In contrast, we learn a universal prior from over a thousand
clothed humans to achieve instant feedforward generation and zero-shot
generalization. Specifically, instead of rigging the avatar with shared
skinning weights, we jointly infer personalized avatar shape, skinning weights,
and pose-dependent deformations, which effectively improves overall geometric
fidelity and reduces deformation artifacts. Moreover, to normalize pose
variations and resolve coupled ambiguity between canonical shapes and skinning
weights, we design a 3D canonicalization process to produce pixel-aligned
initial conditions, which helps to reconstruct fine-grained geometric details.
We then propose a multi-frame feature aggregation to robustly reduce artifacts
introduced in canonicalization and fuse a plausible avatar preserving
person-specific identities. Finally, we train the model in an end-to-end
framework on a large-scale capture dataset, which contains diverse human
subjects paired with high-quality 3D scans. Extensive experiments show that our
method generates more authentic reconstruction and animation than
state-of-the-arts, and can be directly generalized to inputs from casually
taken phone photos. Project page and code is available at
this https URL

author_info.institution: Research Scientist, Codec Avatars Lab, Meta

title: FRESA: Feedforward Reconstruction of Personalized Skinned Avatars from  Few Images

abstract: We introduce X-Dyna, a novel zero-shot, diffusion-based pipeline for animating a single human image using facial expressions and body movements derived from a driving video, that generates realistic, context-aware dynamics for both the subject and the surrounding environment. Building on prior approaches centered on human pose control, X-Dyna addresses key shortcomings causing the loss of dynamic details, enhancing the lifelike qualities of human video animations. At the core of our approach is the Dynamics-Adapter, a lightweight module that effectively integrates reference appearance context into the spatial attentions of the diffusion backbone while preserving the capacity of motion modules in synthesizing fluid and intricate dynamic details. Beyond body pose control, we connect a local control module with our model to capture identity-disentangled facial expressions, facilitating accurate expression transfer for enhanced realism in animated scenes. Together, these components form a unified framework capable of learning physical human motion and natural scene dynamics from a diverse blend of human and scene videos. Comprehensive qualitative and quantitative evaluations demonstrate that X-Dyna outperforms state-of-the-art methods, creating highly lifelike and expressive animations. The code is available at this https URL.
title: X-Dyna: Expressive Dynamic Human Image Animation

abstract: Virtual try-on (VTON) technology has gained attention due to its potential to transform online retail by enabling realistic clothing visualization of images and videos. However, most existing methods struggle to achieve high-quality results across image and video try-on tasks, especially in long video scenarios. In this work, we introduce CatV2TON, a simple and effective vision-based virtual try-on (V2TON) method that supports both image and video try-on tasks with a single diffusion transformer model. By temporally concatenating garment and person inputs and training on a mix of image and video datasets, CatV2TON achieves robust try-on performance across static and dynamic settings. For efficient long-video generation, we propose an overlapping clip-based inference strategy that uses sequential frame guidance and Adaptive Clip Normalization (AdaCN) to maintain temporal consistency with reduced resource demands. We also present ViViD-S, a refined video try-on dataset, achieved by filtering back-facing frames and applying 3D mask smoothing for enhanced temporal consistency. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that CatV2TON outperforms existing methods in both image and video try-on tasks, offering a versatile and reliable solution for realistic virtual try-ons across diverse scenarios.

keyInsights: A single Diffusion Transformer can unify image and video virtual try-on tasks by adapting inputs through a temporal concatenation strategy.
Maintaining temporal coherence in long video generation requires segment-based processing with mechanisms like Adaptive Clip Normalization to ensure consistent feature statistics across transitions.
Refining training datasets, particularly for video, by addressing issues like pose diversity and mask consistency, is crucial for improving the realism and robustness of generative models.
originalProblem: Existing virtual try-on methods are often specialized for either static images or dynamic videos, leading to distinct model designs and inconsistent performance across tasks.
Video-based try-on typically suffers from issues like flickering, inconsistent garment deformation, and color shifts, particularly in long sequences, while also being computationally intensive.

Current video try-on datasets exhibit limitations such as a lack of diverse human poses (e.g., back views) and temporal discontinuities in clothing-agnostic masks, hindering model training and realism.
results: CatV²TON achieved state-of-the-art quantitative performance on image-based try-on datasets (VITON-HD, DressCode) with improved SSIM and LPIPS scores and competitive FID/KID scores.

The model significantly outperformed existing video-based try-on methods on VVT and ViViD-S datasets across all video quality metrics (VFID_I, VFID_R, SSIM, LPIPS), generating videos with superior temporal coherence.

Ablation studies confirmed the essential contributions of the PoseEncoder for handling complex poses and AdaCN for mitigating flickering and color mismatches in long video sequences, along with the benefits of the refined ViViD-S dataset.
solution: A unified Diffusion Transformer (DiT) model is introduced, utilizing a temporal concatenation input strategy to process both single images and video sequences for try-on.

An Overlapping Clip-Based Inference strategy with Adaptive Clip Normalization (AdaCN) is developed to generate long, temporally coherent videos by segmenting generation and correcting feature statistics between clips.
The ViViD-S dataset is curated by filtering suboptimal human orientations and applying 3D mask smoothing to clothing-agnostic masks, enhancing data quality for video try-on training.
paper_summary.summary: CatV²TON presents a unified diffusion transformer model that seamlessly performs both image-based and video-based virtual try-on. It achieves state-of-the-art results across quantitative metrics for both tasks and substantially improves temporal consistency in long try-on videos through novel inference strategies and efficient training.

title: CatV2TON: Taming Diffusion Transformers for Vision-Based Virtual Try-On with Temporal Concatenation

abstract: Character animation aims to generate lifelike videos by transferring motion dynamics from a driving video to a reference image. Recent strides in generative models have paved the way for high-fidelity character animation. In this work, we present Kling-MotionControl, a unified DiT-based framework engineered specifically for robust, precise, and expressive holistic character animation. Leveraging a divide-and-conquer strategy within a cohesive system, the model orchestrates heterogeneous motion representations tailored to the distinct characteristics of body, face, and hands, effectively reconciling large-scale structural stability with fine-grained articulatory expressiveness. To ensure robust cross-identity generalization, we incorporate adaptive identity-agnostic learning, facilitating natural motion retargeting for diverse characters ranging from realistic humans to stylized cartoons. Simultaneously, we guarantee faithful appearance preservation through meticulous identity injection and fusion designs, further supported by a subject library mechanism that leverages comprehensive reference contexts. To ensure practical utility, we implement an advanced acceleration framework utilizing multi-stage distillation, boosting inference speed by over 10x. Kling-MotionControl distinguishes itself through intelligent semantic motion understanding and precise text responsiveness, allowing for flexible control beyond visual inputs. Human preference evaluations demonstrate that Kling-MotionControl delivers superior performance compared to leading commercial and open-source solutions, achieving exceptional fidelity in holistic motion control, open domain generalization, and visual quality and coherence. These results establish Kling-MotionControl as a robust solution for high-quality, controllable, and lifelike character animation.

keyInsights: Orchestrating heterogeneous motion representations within a unified DiT framework effectively balances structural stability for large movements and fine-grained expressiveness for delicate details like micro-expressions and finger articulation.

Decoupling motion dynamics from specific identity attributes at a geometric level, combined with semantic motion modeling, enables robust motion transfer across widely varying character morphologies without identity degradation.

Advanced inference acceleration techniques, including efficient dual-branch sampling and multi-stage distillation, can yield over 10x speedup for complex video generation while maintaining high-fidelity output.
originalProblem: Prior methods struggled to simultaneously manage large-scale body movements and fine-grained facial expressions/hand gestures in character animation.
Limitations existed in robustly transferring motion across characters with significantly different morphologies (cross-identity generalization) without identity drift or visual artifacts.
The high computational cost of generating high-resolution, temporally coherent animated videos impeded practical deployment and efficiency.
results: Kling-MotionControl consistently surpassed leading commercial and open-source solutions in human preference evaluations across all dimensions, achieving an 'Overall Preference' score of 16.25 against Runway Act-Two and 4.00 against Wan-Animate.

The framework demonstrated superior visual quality, precise multi-granularity motion control (expression and motion accuracy), and robust identity preservation, even in challenging scenarios with complex poses or cross-identity transfer.
An end-to-end inference speed boost exceeding 10x was achieved, significantly lowering deployment costs without compromising model performance.
solution: A unified Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based framework employs a "divide-and-conquer" strategy using heterogeneous motion representations tailored for body, face, and hands, integrated via progressive multi-stage training.
Adaptive cross-identity motion transfer is achieved through identity-agnostic motion learning and a semantic motion modeling module that decouples motion dynamics from physical attributes.

Faithful identity preservation is ensured by dedicated identity encoding and a Subject Library mechanism, complemented by high-efficiency inference acceleration techniques like dual-branch sampling and multi-stage distillation for over 10x speedup.
paper_summary.summary: Kling-MotionControl, developed by the Kling Team at Kuaishou Technology, introduces a unified Diffusion Transformer framework for adaptive holistic character animation, orchestrating multi-granularity motions from a reference image and driving video. The system demonstrates superior visual quality, precise motion control, and robust identity preservation across diverse characters, while also achieving over 10x inference acceleration compared to previous methods.

title: Kling-MotionControl Technical Report

abstract: Given a pair of images depicting a person and a garment separately, image-based 3D virtual try-on methods aim to reconstruct a 3D human model that realistically portrays the person wearing the desired garment. In this paper, we present IPVTON, a novel image-based 3D virtual try-on framework. IPVTON employs score distillation sampling with image prompts to optimize a hybrid 3D human representation, integrating target garment features into diffusion priors through an image prompt adapter. To avoid interference with non-target areas, we leverage mask-guided image prompt embeddings to focus the image features on the try-on regions. Moreover, we impose geometric constraints on the 3D model with a pseudo silhouette generated by ControlNet, ensuring that the clothed 3D human model retains the shape of the source identity while accurately wearing the target garments. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that IPVTON outperforms previous methods in image-based 3D virtual try-on tasks, excelling in both geometry and texture.
title: IPVTON: Image-based 3D Virtual Try-on with Image Prompt Adapter

abstract:
3D human reconstruction and animation are long-standing topics in computer
graphics and vision. However, existing methods typically rely on sophisticated
dense-view capture and/or time-consuming per-subject optimization procedures.
To address these limitations, we propose HumanRAM, a novel feed-forward
approach for generalizable human reconstruction and animation from monocular or
sparse human images. Our approach integrates human reconstruction and animation
into a unified framework by introducing explicit pose conditions, parameterized
by a shared SMPL-X neural texture, into transformer-based large reconstruction
models (LRM). Given monocular or sparse input images with associated camera
parameters and SMPL-X poses, our model employs scalable transformers and a
DPT-based decoder to synthesize realistic human renderings under novel
viewpoints and novel poses. By leveraging the explicit pose conditions, our
model simultaneously enables high-quality human reconstruction and
high-fidelity pose-controlled animation. Experiments show that HumanRAM
significantly surpasses previous methods in terms of reconstruction accuracy,
animation fidelity, and generalization performance on real-world datasets.
Video results are available at this https URL

organization_info.name: State Key Laboratory of CAD&CG, Zhejiang University

keyInsights: Integrating a shared, learnable neural texture with the SMPL-X model effectively provides generalizable human-specific priors for both reconstruction and animation.
Directly guiding a transformer with rasterized 2D "pose images" derived from a posed SMPL-X neural texture enables implicit 3D understanding for human forms.
Using a Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT)-based decoder with residual CNN layers and skip connections enhances local detail and reduces artifacts in transformer-generated pixel outputs.
originalProblem: Creating realistic 3D human avatars from limited input images is challenging and often requires expensive multi-camera setups and time-consuming optimization.
Existing large reconstruction models (LRMs) lack fine-grained detail in complex human geometry, primarily focus on static reconstruction, and struggle with diverse poses and clothing.
Traditional 3D human modeling approaches typically involve lengthy per-subject optimization procedures, limiting accessibility.
results: Generates photo-realistic 3D human renderings and animations from as few as one input image, demonstrating high fidelity.
Outperforms existing methods on synthetic datasets (THuman2.1, Human4DiT) with higher PSNR and SSIM scores and lower LPIPS scores, while also generalizing effectively to real-world data.
Achieves superior animation quality, preserving photo-realistic appearance and handling complex pose deformations more effectively than baseline methods.
solution: A unified feed-forward transformer-based architecture performs both 3D human reconstruction from sparse views and animation to novel poses in a single pass.
A learnable neural texture integrated with the SMPL-X parametric human model provides explicit human pose and geometric conditions for the transformer.
A Dense Prediction Transformer (DPT)-based decoder is incorporated to refine generated images, reducing artifacts and improving local detail.
paper_summary.summary: HumanRAM is a unified feed-forward transformer-based framework that reconstructs 3D human avatars from sparse input images and animates them into new poses in a single pass. The method generates photo-realistic human renderings from as few as one input image, achieving high fidelity and generalizing well.

title: HumanRAM: Feed-forward Human Reconstruction and Animation Model using  Transformers

abstract: We introduce Dream, Lift, Animate (DLA), a novel framework that reconstructs animatable 3D human avatars from a single image. This is achieved by leveraging multi-view generation, 3D Gaussian lifting, and pose-aware UV-space mapping of 3D Gaussians. Given an image, we first dream plausible multi-views using a video diffusion model, capturing rich geometric and appearance details. These views are then lifted into unstructured 3D Gaussians. To enable animation, we propose a transformer-based encoder that models global spatial relationships and projects these Gaussians into a structured latent representation aligned with the UV space of a parametric body model. This latent code is decoded into UV-space Gaussians that can be animated via body-driven deformation and rendered conditioned on pose and viewpoint. By anchoring Gaussians to the UV manifold, our method ensures consistency during animation while preserving fine visual details. DLA enables real-time rendering and intuitive editing without requiring post-processing. Our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches on the ActorsHQ and 4D-Dress datasets in both perceptual quality and photometric accuracy. By combining the generative strengths of video diffusion models with a pose-aware UV-space Gaussian mapping, DLA bridges the gap between unstructured 3D representations and high-fidelity, animation-ready avatars.

author_info.institution: the Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Decomposing the problem into 'Dream, Lift, Animate' effectively tackles the challenges of missing data, 3D coherence, and animatability by leveraging diffusion models for view generation and structured latent spaces for animation.
Mapping unstructured 3D Gaussians to a UV-aligned latent representation via a transformer-based encoder provides a consistent and deformable topology essential for robust animation.

A pose- and view-conditioned Gaussian Parameter Decoder (GPD) allows for realistic pose-dependent effects and view-dependent shading while maintaining real-time rendering efficiency through a cached canonical component.
originalProblem: Reconstructing complete 3D human avatars from a single 2D image, which inherently lacks information about occluded or unseen body parts.
Ensuring geometrically coherent and high-fidelity 3D models while enabling realistic animation under novel poses and viewpoints without visual artifacts.
Addressing the computational inefficiency and lack of real-time performance in many existing high-quality 3D avatar generation methods.
results: DLA achieves state-of-the-art quantitative performance, demonstrating an LPIPS score of 0.0580 for novel view synthesis and 0.0471 for novel pose synthesis on the ActorsHQ dataset, outperforming comparable methods.

The framework produces high-fidelity, photorealistic 3D human avatars that exhibit smooth, artifact-free animation under various novel poses and viewpoints, and generalizes well to 'in-the-wild' images.

It enables real-time rendering at 33 FPS for 512x512 resolution and supports practical applications such as semantic avatar editing and latent space interpolation, showcasing a meaningful and well-structured latent space.
solution: A multi-stage pipeline where a ControlNet-guided video diffusion model first generates consistent multi-view observations to hallucinate missing appearance and geometric details.

The generated multi-views are lifted into an intermediate unstructured 3D Gaussian representation, which is then mapped by a transformer-based encoder into a structured, UV-aligned latent avatar code.

A Gaussian Parameter Decoder (GPD), with canonical and pose/view-dependent branches, decodes the latent code into an animatable Gaussian-based avatar, leveraging linear blend skinning for deformation and Gaussian Splatting for real-time rendering.
paper_summary.summary: The Dream, Lift, Animate (DLA) framework, developed by researchers from ETH Zurich, NVIDIA, and The Chinese University of Hong Kong, reconstructs high-fidelity, animatable 3D human avatars from a single RGB image. It achieves superior performance in novel view and pose synthesis, reaching an LPIPS of 0.0471 for novel pose on ActorsHQ, and renders in real-time at 33 FPS for 512x512 resolution.

title: Dream, Lift, Animate: From Single Images to Animatable Gaussian Avatars

abstract: Recent diffusion methods have made significant progress in generating videos from single images due to their powerful visual generation capabilities. However, challenges persist in image-to-video synthesis, particularly in human video generation, where inferring view-consistent, motion-dependent clothing wrinkles from a single image remains a formidable problem. In this paper, we present Human Video Generation in 4D (HVG), a latent video diffusion model capable of generating high-quality, multi-view, spatiotemporally coherent human videos from a single image with 3D pose and view control. HVG achieves this through three key designs: (i) Articulated Pose Modulation, which captures the anatomical relationships of 3D joints via a novel dual-dimensional bone map and resolves self-occlusions across views by introducing 3D information; (ii) View and Temporal Alignment, which ensures multi-view consistency and alignment between a reference image and pose sequences for frame-to-frame stability; and (iii) Progressive Spatio-Temporal Sampling with temporal alignment to maintain smooth transitions in long multi-view animations. Extensive experiments on image-to-video tasks demonstrate that HVG outperforms existing methods in generating high-quality 4D human videos from diverse human images and pose inputs.

keyInsights: Dual-dimensional bone maps effectively represent volumetric human anatomy, proving superior to 2D skeletons or simplified 3D meshes for accurate pose and occlusion handling.
Spatially normalizing human figures across different views allows for efficient cross-view consistency within a 2D attention mechanism, avoiding the high computational cost of full 3D attention.
Progressive spatio-temporal sampling with weighted fusion is essential for generating long, coherent multi-view videos, mitigating inconsistencies that arise in extended sequences.
originalProblem: Existing video diffusion models often produce unnatural or inconsistent articulated human motion, particularly for complex movements.
Prior 2D pose-guided human animation methods are limited to fixed camera viewpoints and lack anatomical context, leading to implausible deformations from novel angles.
3D parametric models like SMPL, while providing some geometric representation, can oversimplify character-specific details, causing "shape leakage" and distortions during multi-view synthesis.
results: HVG achieved superior quantitative metrics for novel view synthesis, with a FID of 59.35 (compared to baseline LHM's 81.60) and FVD of 152.1 (compared to LHM's 248.4).
For the more complex task of novel view and pose synthesis, HVG demonstrated dominance with a FID of 84.74 (vs. LHM's 103.7) and FVD of 177.4 (vs. LHM's 258.8).

Qualitatively, the model generates highly realistic and anatomically consistent human videos, effectively preserving input identity, handling occlusions, and rendering detailed textures and clothing dynamics across diverse poses and views.
solution: A novel dual-dimensional bone map, comprising depth and normal maps derived from 3D ellipsoids, provides robust pose guidance that captures volumetric structure and anatomical relationships.
An efficient view alignment strategy normalizes human positions across views, enabling lightweight cross-view consistency, complemented by temporal alignment for frame-to-frame stability.
A progressive spatio-temporal sampling technique generates long, multi-view videos by processing overlapping segments with weighted fusion, ensuring global coherence during inference.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from UC Merced and Stability AI developed Human Video Generation in 4D (HVG), a latent video diffusion model that synthesizes high-quality, multi-view, and spatiotemporally coherent human videos from a single image using 3D pose and camera viewpoint control. The model achieves a FID of 59.35 and an FVD of 152.1 for novel view synthesis, outperforming previous methods.

title: Human Video Generation from a Single Image with 3D Pose and View Control

abstract: Recent advances in diffusion models have greatly improved pose-driven character animation. However, existing methods are limited to spatially aligned reference-pose pairs with matched skeletal structures. Handling reference-pose misalignment remains unsolved. To address this, we present One-to-All Animation, a unified framework for high-fidelity character animation and image pose transfer for references with arbitrary layouts. First, to handle spatially misaligned reference, we reformulate training as a self-supervised outpainting task that transforms diverse-layout reference into a unified occluded-input format. Second, to process partially visible reference, we design a reference extractor for comprehensive identity feature extraction. Further, we integrate hybrid reference fusion attention to handle varying resolutions and dynamic sequence lengths. Finally, from the perspective of generation quality, we introduce identity-robust pose control that decouples appearance from skeletal structure to mitigate pose overfitting, and a token replace strategy for coherent long-video generation. Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing approaches. The code and model are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Framing character animation as an outpainting task, with masked reference images, allows models to inherently learn to handle spatial misalignment without explicit alignment steps.
Decoupling facial identity from the driving pose, even by intentionally introducing skeletal inconsistencies during training, improves identity preservation and prevents "pose overfitting."

Careful design of attention mechanisms, specifically using RoPE_3D with f=0 in cross-attention, enables a single model to perform both image and video generation tasks and extrapolate to longer sequences.
originalProblem: Existing pose-driven character animation methods struggle with spatial layout mismatches and facial inconsistencies between reference images and driving videos.
Traditional approaches assume shared layouts and skeletons, leading to visual artifacts and identity degradation when these conditions are not met.
Most prior solutions are task-specific, unable to simultaneously handle unaligned image-to-image pose transfer and image-to-video animation.
results: The One-to-All-1.3B and One-to-All-14B models achieved superior PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, FID, FID-VID, and FVD scores on TikTok and Cartoon datasets, surpassing existing DiT-based models.
It obtained the lowest FID and LPIPS scores on the DeepFashion dataset for image pose transfer, indicating better perceptual quality.

User studies demonstrated a clear preference for the proposed method, with users favoring its "Unseen region quality" (50.8% win) and "Seen region fidelity" (69.2% win) over the current state-of-the-art in misaligned animation.
solution: The framework reformulates training as an outpainting problem, using face-centered random masking to simulate spatial mismatches during self-supervised learning.
A dedicated Reference Extractor with Hybrid Reference Fusion Attention (HRFA) captures robust identity features from occluded references, leveraging RoPE_3D with f=0 for temporal extrapolation.
Identity-Robust Pose Control decouples facial identity from the driving pose skeleton, enhancing consistency through face region enhancement and reference-guided pose conditioning.
paper_summary.summary: The "One-to-All Animation" framework presents a unified approach for character animation and image pose transfer, handling significant spatial and facial misalignments between reference images and driving motions. It achieves superior quantitative metrics on video and image datasets while maintaining identity consistency and generating coherent long videos, outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods in user studies.

title: One-to-All Animation: Alignment-Free Character Animation and Image Pose Transfer

abstract: We tackle the task of any-reference video generation, which aims to synthesize videos conditioned on arbitrary types and combinations of reference subjects, together with textual prompts. This task faces persistent challenges, including identity inconsistency, entanglement among multiple reference subjects, and copy-paste artifacts. To address these issues, we introduce MAGREF, a unified and effective framework for any-reference video generation. Our approach incorporates masked guidance and a subject disentanglement mechanism, enabling flexible synthesis conditioned on diverse reference images and textual prompts. Specifically, masked guidance employs a region-aware masking mechanism combined with pixel-wise channel concatenation to preserve appearance features of multiple subjects along the channel dimension. This design preserves identity consistency and maintains the capabilities of the pre-trained backbone, without requiring any architectural changes. To mitigate subject confusion, we introduce a subject disentanglement mechanism which injects the semantic values of each subject derived from the text condition into its corresponding visual region. Additionally, we establish a four-stage data pipeline to construct diverse training pairs, effectively alleviating copy-paste artifacts. Extensive experiments on a comprehensive benchmark demonstrate that MAGREF consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches, paving the way for scalable, controllable, and high-fidelity any-reference video synthesis. Code and model can be found at: this https URL
title: MAGREF: Masked Guidance for Any-Reference Video Generation with Subject Disentanglement

abstract: Video Virtual Try-On (VVT) aims to synthesize garments that appear natural across consecutive video frames, capturing both their dynamics and interactions with human motion. Despite recent progress, existing VVT methods still suffer from inadequate garment fidelity and limited spatiotemporal consistency. The reasons are: (1) under-exploitation of garment information, with limited garment cues being injected, resulting in weaker fine-detail fidelity; and (2) a lack of spatiotemporal modeling, which hampers cross-frame identity consistency and causes temporal jitter and appearance drift. In this paper, we present MagicTryOn, a diffusion-transformer based framework for garment-preserving video virtual try-on. To preserve fine-grained garment details, we propose a fine-grained garment-preservation strategy that disentangles garment cues and injects these decomposed priors into the denoising process. To improve temporal garment consistency and suppress jitter, we introduce a garment-aware spatiotemporal rotary positional embedding (RoPE) that extends RoPE within full self-attention, using spatiotemporal relative positions to modulate garment tokens. We further impose a mask-aware loss during training to enhance fidelity within garment regions. Moreover, we adopt distribution-matching distillation to compress the sampling trajectory to four steps, enabling real-time inference without degrading garment fidelity. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that MagicTryOn outperforms existing methods, delivering superior garment-detail fidelity and temporal stability in unconstrained settings.
title: MagicTryOn: Harnessing Diffusion Transformer for Garment-Preserving Video Virtual Try-on

abstract:
This paper studies the human image animation task, which aims to generate a
video of a certain reference identity following a particular motion sequence.
Existing animation works typically employ the frame-warping technique to
animate the reference image towards the target motion. Despite achieving
reasonable results, these approaches face challenges in maintaining temporal
consistency throughout the animation due to the lack of temporal modeling and
poor preservation of reference identity. In this work, we introduce
MagicAnimate, a diffusion-based framework that aims at enhancing temporal
consistency, preserving reference image faithfully, and improving animation
fidelity. To achieve this, we first develop a video diffusion model to encode
temporal information. Second, to maintain the appearance coherence across
frames, we introduce a novel appearance encoder to retain the intricate details
of the reference image. Leveraging these two innovations, we further employ a
simple video fusion technique to encourage smooth transitions for long video
animation. Empirical results demonstrate the superiority of our method over
baseline approaches on two benchmarks. Notably, our approach outperforms the
strongest baseline by over 38% in terms of video fidelity on the challenging
TikTok dancing dataset. Code and model will be made available.

keyInsights: Integrating temporal attention blocks into a video diffusion model is essential for achieving high temporal consistency and eliminating flickering in animated sequences.
A dense appearance encoder, rather than sparse global features, is critical for faithfully preserving intricate details of the reference identity and background.
Simple video fusion via averaging overlapping predictions across segments effectively ensures smooth transitions and long-range coherence for extended animations.
originalProblem: Many current human image animation methods produce videos with temporal flickering and incoherence due to frame-by-frame generation.
Existing approaches often fail to faithfully preserve the fine-grained details of the reference identity, including facial features, clothing, and background elements.
Generating long, smooth animation sequences from a single reference image and motion sequence is challenging, often resulting in discontinuities.
results: MagicAnimate achieves state-of-the-art quantitative performance, reducing FID-VID by 63.7% and FVD by 38.8% compared to the strongest diffusion baseline (DisCo) on the TikTok dataset.

Qualitative evaluations demonstrate highly temporally consistent animations that faithfully preserve fine-grained identity details, background, and object textures, outperforming prior GAN and diffusion-based methods.

The framework robustly generalizes to unseen artistic domains, integrates with text-to-image models, and supports multi-person animation, with ablation studies confirming the efficacy of its core components.
solution: A 3D temporal UNet is employed, integrating temporal attention blocks to model inter-frame dependencies and ensure temporal consistency across video segments.
A novel appearance encoder extracts dense, fine-grained visual features from the reference image, which are then integrated into the diffusion model to preserve identity and background details.
Motion is guided by DensePose, and a simple video fusion technique based on a sliding window approach enables smooth and coherent generation of arbitrarily long animation sequences.
paper_summary.summary: MagicAnimate presents a diffusion-based framework for generating temporally consistent human image animations from a single reference image and a motion sequence. The approach yields state-of-the-art results, demonstrating superior video fidelity and identity preservation on challenging datasets like TikTok and TED-talks, and generalizes effectively to various artistic styles and multi-person scenarios.

title:
MagicAnimate: Temporally Consistent Human Image Animation using
  Diffusion Model

abstract:
Recent advances in large models have significantly advanced image-to-3D
reconstruction. However, the generated models are often fused into a single
piece, limiting their applicability in downstream tasks. This paper focuses on
3D garment generation, a key area for applications like virtual try-on with
dynamic garment animations, which require garments to be separable and
simulation-ready. We introduce Dress-1-to-3, a novel pipeline that reconstructs
physics-plausible, simulation-ready separated garments with sewing patterns and
humans from an in-the-wild image. Starting with the image, our approach
combines a pre-trained image-to-sewing pattern generation model for creating
coarse sewing patterns with a pre-trained multi-view diffusion model to produce
multi-view images. The sewing pattern is further refined using a differentiable
garment simulator based on the generated multi-view images. Versatile
experiments demonstrate that our optimization approach substantially enhances
the geometric alignment of the reconstructed 3D garments and humans with the
input image. Furthermore, by integrating a texture generation module and a
human motion generation module, we produce customized physics-plausible and
realistic dynamic garment demonstrations. Project page:
this https URL
title: Dress-1-to-3: Single Image to Simulation-Ready 3D Outfit with Diffusion  Prior and Differentiable Physics

abstract: Recent diffusion-based human image animation techniques have demonstrated impressive success in synthesizing videos that faithfully follow a given reference identity and a sequence of desired movement poses. Despite this, there are still two limitations: i) an extra reference model is required to align the identity image with the main video branch, which significantly increases the optimization burden and model parameters; ii) the generated video is usually short in time (e.g., 24 frames), hampering practical applications. To address these shortcomings, we present a UniAnimate framework to enable efficient and long-term human video generation. First, to reduce the optimization difficulty and ensure temporal coherence, we map the reference image along with the posture guidance and noise video into a common feature space by incorporating a unified video diffusion model. Second, we propose a unified noise input that supports random noised input as well as first frame conditioned input, which enhances the ability to generate long-term video. Finally, to further efficiently handle long sequences, we explore an alternative temporal modeling architecture based on state space model to replace the original computation-consuming temporal Transformer. Extensive experimental results indicate that UniAnimate achieves superior synthesis results over existing state-of-the-art counterparts in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations. Notably, UniAnimate can even generate highly consistent one-minute videos by iteratively employing the first frame conditioning strategy. Code and models will be publicly available. Project page: this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Integrating appearance and motion processing within a single unified network, alongside explicit reference pose guidance, leads to more robust identity preservation and simplified model optimization.
Iteratively conditioning subsequent video segments on the previous segment's final frame effectively overcomes temporal discontinuities and enables the generation of much longer, coherent videos.

State Space Models (SSMs), specifically temporal Mamba, can efficiently model long-range temporal dependencies with linear complexity, offering a scalable alternative to Transformers for video generation tasks.
originalProblem: Existing diffusion models for human image animation rely on an extra reference network, increasing architectural complexity and optimization difficulty.
Temporal Transformers, commonly used, have quadratic computational complexity, severely limiting generated video length (typically 24 frames) and causing inconsistencies when stitching segments.
Achieving consistent identity and smooth transitions across extended video sequences remains a challenge, with current methods often suffering from artifacts and appearance drifts.
results: UniAnimate consistently outperformed state-of-the-art methods on the TikTok dataset, achieving the lowest Fréchet Video Distance (FVD) of 148.06 and best metrics across L1, PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS.
Ablation studies confirmed that the Unified VDM and explicit reference pose are crucial, with their removal significantly degrading performance (e.g., FVD increased to 205.28 without unified VDM).

Temporal Mamba showed comparable performance to Transformers but with significantly lower memory consumption for long sequences, processing 360 frames (78.8GB) where Transformer-based models ran out of memory (OOM) on an 80G A100 GPU.
solution: A Unified Video Diffusion Model (Unified VDM) that integrates reference image features and noised video latents into a single 3D-UNet, reducing parameters and streamlining optimization.

A 'unified noised input' strategy with first-frame conditioning enables iterative generation of long videos by using the last generated frame as the starting point for the next segment, ensuring seamless transitions.
Replaced computationally intensive temporal Transformers with temporal Mamba modules, providing linear-time complexity for efficient and scalable long-range temporal modeling.
paper_summary.summary: UniAnimate introduces a framework for consistent human image animation, generating long-duration videos from a single reference image and pose sequence. It achieves this by unifying the appearance and motion modeling into a single 3D-UNet and leveraging a temporal Mamba architecture for efficient, long-range temporal coherence, producing one-minute videos with superior fidelity and consistency compared to prior methods.

title: UniAnimate: Taming Unified Video Diffusion Models for Consistent Human Image Animation

abstract: We introduce Gaussian Wardrobe, a novel framework to digitalize compositional 3D neural avatars from multi-view videos. Existing methods for 3D neural avatars typically treat the human body and clothing as an inseparable entity. However, this paradigm fails to capture the dynamics of complex free-form garments and limits the reuse of clothing across different individuals. To overcome these problems, we develop a novel, compositional 3D Gaussian representation to build avatars from multiple layers of free-form garments. The core of our method is decomposing neural avatars into bodies and layers of shape-agnostic neural garments. To achieve this, our framework learns to disentangle each garment layer from multi-view videos and canonicalizes it into a shape-independent space. In experiments, our method models photorealistic avatars with high-fidelity dynamics, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on novel pose synthesis benchmarks. In addition, we demonstrate that the learned compositional garments contribute to a versatile digital wardrobe, enabling a practical virtual try-on application where clothing can be freely transferred to new subjects. Project page: this https URL
title: Gaussian Wardrobe: Compositional 3D Gaussian Avatars for Free-Form Virtual Try-On

abstract:
We present SkyReels-A1, a simple yet effective framework built upon video
diffusion Transformer to facilitate portrait image animation. Existing
methodologies still encounter issues, including identity distortion, background
instability, and unrealistic facial dynamics, particularly in head-only
animation scenarios. Besides, extending to accommodate diverse body proportions
usually leads to visual inconsistencies or unnatural articulations. To address
these challenges, SkyReels-A1 capitalizes on the strong generative capabilities
of video DiT, enhancing facial motion transfer precision, identity retention,
and temporal coherence. The system incorporates an expression-aware
conditioning module that enables seamless video synthesis driven by
expression-guided landmark inputs. Integrating the facial image-text alignment
module strengthens the fusion of facial attributes with motion trajectories,
reinforcing identity preservation. Additionally, SkyReels-A1 incorporates a
multi-stage training paradigm to incrementally refine the correlation between
expressions and motion while ensuring stable identity reproduction. Extensive
empirical evaluations highlight the model's ability to produce visually
coherent and compositionally diverse results, making it highly applicable to
domains such as virtual avatars, remote communication, and digital media
generation.

keyInsights: Utilizing high-precision 3D facial landmarks, extracted via a neural rendering approach, significantly enhances the accuracy and realism of transferring nuanced facial expressions.
Projecting facial identity features into the text feature space of a pre-trained DiT effectively maintains consistent identity throughout animation with minimal parameter overhead.
A multi-stage training strategy, gradually refining motion, identity, and then jointly optimizing all components, is crucial for achieving stable, high-fidelity, and generalized portrait animation.
originalProblem: Existing methods struggled with identity distortion and background instability in animated portraits, leading to visual artifacts.
Current techniques often produced rigid or unnatural facial expressions, failing to capture subtle nuances, especially in head-only animations.
Many prior approaches faced limitations when extending animation capabilities to diverse body proportions (e.g., half-body or full-body), resulting in visual inconsistencies.
results: Achieved an FID score of 59.6884, demonstrating superior image quality compared to open-source baselines (e.g., Follow-Your-Emoji: 100.2192, LivePortrait: 83.3168) and competitive with commercial solutions.

Maintained strong identity preservation with FaceSim-Arc/Cur scores of 0.7196 / 0.7314, comparable to leading baselines, and effectively transferred expressions and pose with competitive L1 differences.

Qualitative results and a user study (63% preference for SkyReels-A1) confirmed its ability to capture subtle expressions, enable robust motion transfer, and support seamless full-body animation across diverse body compositions.
solution: A video diffusion Transformers (DiT) architecture, conditioned on text embeddings and novel 3D Facial Expressions landmarks, extracts high-precision 3D keypoints for expressive motion transfer.
A Facial Image-Text Alignment Module maps facial features from a vision encoder into the text feature space via a lightweight MLP, ensuring robust identity preservation without complex adaptations.

A progressive training paradigm is employed, consisting of motion-driven training, identity-preserving training, and multi-module joint fine-tuning, along with a Face-Aware Loss weighted by optical flow for dynamic regions.
paper_summary.summary: Skywork AI developed SkyReels-A1, a video diffusion transformer framework that generates high-fidelity and expressive portrait animations. It achieves strong identity preservation and realistic facial dynamics across diverse body proportions through expression-aware conditioning and a multi-stage training paradigm, outperforming open-source methods and competing with commercial products.

title: SkyReels-A1: Expressive Portrait Animation in Video Diffusion  Transformers

abstract: Character image animation aims to synthesize high-fidelity videos by transferring motion from a driving sequence to a static reference image. Despite recent advancements, existing methods suffer from two fundamental challenges: (1) suboptimal motion injection strategies that lead to a trade-off between identity preservation and motion consistency, manifesting as a "see-saw", and (2) an over-reliance on explicit pose priors (e.g., skeletons), which inadequately capture intricate dynamics and hinder generalization to arbitrary, non-humanoid characters. To address these challenges, we present DreamActor-M2, a universal animation framework that reimagines motion conditioning as an in-context learning problem. Our approach follows a two-stage paradigm. First, we bridge the input modality gap by fusing reference appearance and motion cues into a unified latent space, enabling the model to jointly reason about spatial identity and temporal dynamics by leveraging the generative prior of foundational models. Second, we introduce a self-bootstrapped data synthesis pipeline that curates pseudo cross-identity training pairs, facilitating a seamless transition from pose-dependent control to direct, end-to-end RGB-driven animation. This strategy significantly enhances generalization across diverse characters and motion scenarios. To facilitate comprehensive evaluation, we further introduce AW Bench, a versatile benchmark encompassing a wide spectrum of characters types and motion scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DreamActor-M2 achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering superior visual fidelity and robust cross-domain generalization. Project Page: this https URL

keyInsights: Spatiotemporal in-context learning effectively leverages pre-trained video diffusion models, allowing them to naturally interpret motion cues as visual context without extensive architectural modifications, bridging modality gaps.

A self-bootstrapped data synthesis pipeline enables a progressive and seamless transition from pose-dependent control to robust, end-to-end RGB-driven animation, addressing the lack of large-scale cross-identity paired data.

Pose augmentation techniques and target-oriented motion-semantic guidance significantly enhance pose-based animation by mitigating identity leakage and improving motion controllability and expressiveness.
originalProblem: Existing motion injection strategies often face a trade-off between preserving character identity and ensuring consistent motion transfer.
Many state-of-the-art animation methods depend heavily on explicit pose priors, limiting generalization to non-humanoid characters and introducing error-proneness.
Current frameworks struggle with universal applicability across diverse character types, complex motion patterns, and multi-subject scenarios.
results: DreamActor-M2 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art visual approaches on the AW Bench, achieving higher scores across imaging quality, motion smoothness, temporal, and appearance consistency in both automatic and human evaluations.

The framework demonstrates competitive performance against industry-level products, matching Kling 2.6's overall quality with a +9.66% GSB lead and significantly outperforming others in subjective comparisons.

It achieves robust cross-domain motion transfer (e.g., human to cartoon), generalizes to diverse character types (animals, objects), varying shot types, and complex multi-person scenarios (one-to-many, multi-to-multi) while maintaining visual fidelity.
solution: The framework reimagines motion conditioning as a spatiotemporal in-context learning problem, where motion signals are concatenated directly with the reference image as a unified input.

A two-stage training paradigm is employed, starting with a pose-based model enhanced by augmented skeletons and target-oriented text guidance, then transitioning to an end-to-end model trained on self-bootstrapped, pseudo-paired RGB data.
A novel and comprehensive benchmark dataset, AW Bench, was introduced to rigorously evaluate universal character animation across a wide array of character types and motion scenarios.
paper_summary.summary: DreamActor-M2 introduces a universal character animation framework utilizing spatiotemporal in-context learning, enabling high-fidelity video synthesis by transferring motion from any driving sequence to any static reference image. The method effectively eliminates reliance on explicit pose priors, achieving robust animation across diverse character types and complex motions.

title: DreamActor-M2: Universal Character Image Animation via Spatiotemporal In-Context Learning

abstract: Pose-guided video generation refers to controlling the motion of subjects in generated video through a sequence of poses. It enables precise control over subject motion and has important applications in animation. However, current pose-guided video generation methods are limited to accepting only human poses as input, thus generalizing poorly to pose of other subjects. To address this issue, we propose PoseAnything, the first universal pose-guided video generation framework capable of handling both human and non-human characters, supporting arbitrary skeletal inputs. To enhance consistency preservation during motion, we introduce Part-aware Temporal Coherence Module, which divides the subject into different parts, establishes part correspondences, and computes cross-attention between corresponding parts across frames to achieve fine-grained part-level consistency. Additionally, we propose Subject and Camera Motion Decoupled CFG, a novel guidance strategy that, for the first time, enables independent camera movement control in pose-guided video generation, by separately injecting subject and camera motion control information into the positive and negative anchors of CFG. Furthermore, we present XPose, a high-quality public dataset containing 50,000 non-human pose-video pairs, along with an automated pipeline for annotation and filtering. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Pose-Anything significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both effectiveness and generalization.

keyInsights: Universal pose guidance for arbitrary skeletal inputs can be achieved by carefully integrating pose latent representations via channel-wise concatenation within a diffusion model.
